---
title: Cityseer Guide
editor:
  render-on-save: true
---

`cityseer` is a Python package for pedestrian-scale network analysis and urban morphology. It computes centrality, accessibility, mixed-use, statistical, and visibility metrics over street networks, using a high-performance `rust` backend so that analyses scale to large cities.

This guide provides tutorials and worked examples to help you get started. If you're looking for the API reference or source code:

- [cityseer API documentation](https://cityseer.benchmarkurbanism.com/)
- [cityseer Github repository](https://github.com/benchmark-urbanism/cityseer-api)

## What can cityseer do?

- **[Network Centrality](recipes/centrality/index.qmd):** Identify the most connected and important streets using shortest-path and simplest-path centrality measures.
- **[Accessibility](recipes/accessibility/index.qmd):** Quantify how easily amenities (shops, parks, restaurants) can be reached from each point in the network.
- **[Statistics](recipes/stats/index.qmd):** Aggregate numeric properties of nearby features (e.g., building heights) over the network.
- **[Visibility](recipes/visibility/index.qmd):** Assess street enclosure and openness from building footprints or terrain data.
- **[Continuity](recipes/continuity/index.qmd):** Measure how consistently named routes and road classifications extend through the network.

## Getting Started

### Python 101

New to Python or computational notebooks? Start with the [Python 101](class/index.qmd) section, which covers the basics of Python, spatial data with Shapely and GeoPandas, and an introduction to urban analytics with `osmnx` and `momepy`.

### Quickstart

Ready to use `cityseer`? The [Getting Started](recipes/quickstart.qmd) page introduces core concepts (primal/dual graphs, NetworkStructure, distance thresholds) and walks through a minimal end-to-end analysis.

### Recipes

For practical, worked examples covering network preparation, centrality, accessibility, statistics, and visibility analysis, head to the [recipes](recipes/index.qmd) section.

::: {.callout-tip}
The recipes assume familiarity with Python, `geopandas`, and projected coordinate reference systems. If any of these are new to you, start with [Python 101](class/index.qmd) first.
:::

### Datasets

The datasets used in the recipes are available on the [datasets](data/datasets.qmd) page, with source links and licensing information.

### Glossary

Unfamiliar with terms like "dual graph" or "edge rolloff"? See the [Glossary](glossary.qmd) for definitions of key concepts.

## Installation

Install `cityseer` with `pip`:

```bash
pip install --upgrade cityseer
```

Or from a notebook:

```python
!pip install --upgrade cityseer
```

The examples in this guide also use packages such as `geopandas`, `networkx`, `osmnx`, `momepy`, `matplotlib`, and `seaborn`. These can be installed in the same way, or you can install all dependencies at once by cloning the [repository](https://github.com/benchmark-urbanism/cityseer-examples) and running `uv sync` (see [uv documentation](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/)).
